story:
Sr. Agatha slammed her palm on the desk to silence the giggles and remind the first graders that their first confession was tomorrow. The words on the blackboard—My First Confession—loomed like a sentence. She warned them not to mention Sputnik again. Dante stared at his desk, avoiding her laser stare. He didn’t want to be called a “spiritual nincompoop” twice. He was seven. And yet he was supposed to figure out sin.

Sputnik filled his mind anyway. It felt like the biggest thing since the war—proof that space wasn’t just movie magic. But tomorrow’s mystery scared him more. How do you confess sins when you don’t even know what counts? The paper Sr. Agatha handed out listed the Ten Commandments and the seven deadly sins. Moses he knew. The rest Sister had to explain aloud—old names, old rules, dead men from church history. How could rules that old still fit boys who had just learned to tie their shoes? He tried to name a sin. Nothing. He was a good kid. What did they want?

At lunch, a girl announced she’d confess to disobeying her parents. “But you didn’t,” Dante objected. She shrugged. “It’s the only way.” That night he stared at the list. So many strange words. Charity, his big sister, perched in the doorway and spooked him with stories: a kid who’d wet his pants in the dark confessional closet, blanket punishments, a friend who kissed a boy after confessing and then panicked when the nuns called French kissing a sin. “Pray no one farts in there before your turn,” Charity added, then warned, “Avoid Fr. Aloysius. He’s the worst.”

Dante wished he could face the day like David facing Goliath. In his mind, David never sinned. So Dante practiced with a slingshot in a vacant lot, teaching other kids too. Still, a slingshot couldn’t help inside a wooden booth.

The next morning, the nuns stood like silent sentries and lined the children up. Fr. Aloysius on the right, Fr. Clarence on the left. Dante prayed for Clarence. No luck. When a weeping girl stepped out, Sr. Agatha nudged him toward Aloysius. The confessional was a cramped, shadowy closet, the little screen hissing open.

“Tell me your sins, boy,” the priest breathed. Dante told the truth: he didn’t have any. The priest didn’t buy it. “No lies? Not even one?” Dante felt ashamed for not being bad. The priest pressed harder—everyone sins; surely Dante had disobeyed his parents; pride itself was a sin. Dante tried to reason it through. If sin is a conscious choice, how could he be guilty of sins he hadn’t chosen? Wasn’t it worse to make something up?

The priest grew stern. Dante tried to understand: when the priest mentioned adultery, Dante blinked. “I’m not an adult,” he muttered, then apologized for sounding smart-alecky. The priest talked about sin as a loose thread that unravels a whole sweater. Dante said it felt like a test you could only pass by getting answers wrong. Finally, the priest gave him a penance: forgive someone who hadn’t asked for, or earned, forgiveness.

Dante stepped out dizzy and relieved into the light spilling through stained glass—saints shining in colors, incense thin in the air, a few people kneeling quiet. Sr. Agatha’s face softened as she nodded at him. He found a pew and knelt. If the priest represented God in there, maybe God could handle honesty out here. Dante folded his hands and did his penance the only way he understood: Father, forgive Fr. Aloysius. He does not know what he’s doing.
